steps.description: State the LHS and the plan
steps.explanation: We start from the left-hand side and head towards $\tan\theta$, converting each double angle into single-angle ratios so a factor cancels.

steps.description: Choose a form of cos 2theta for the numerator
steps.explanation: We pick the $1 - 2\sin^2\theta$ version because subtracting it from $1$ will cancel the constant and leave a pure sine term.
The two $1$'s cancel, so the numerator becomes $2\sin^2\theta$, a single term that shares a factor with the denominator.
The sine double-angle formula rewrites the denominator using single-angle ratios; it contains the factor $2\sin\theta$ that the numerator also has.
With both parts rewritten, the shared factor $2\sin\theta$ is now visible in numerator and denominator.
Dividing top and bottom by $2\sin\theta$ leaves the ratio $\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$.
By definition this ratio is $\tan\theta$, which is exactly the target expression.
The LHS has been reduced to $\tan\theta$, matching the right-hand side.
At $\theta = 30^\circ$ both sides equal $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt3}$, which supports the identity.
The LHS equals the RHS for all valid $\theta$, so the identity is proved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Using $1 - \cos 2\theta = 2\sin^2\theta$ and $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$, the fraction reduces to $\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \tan\theta$, hence $\dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{\sin 2\theta} \equiv \tan\theta$.
